meta.title: Teologia da Libertação — Cronologia
meta.subtitle: Chronology of Liberation Theology (1960s–present)
meta.description: An open, source-referenced chronology of Teologia da Libertação (Liberation Theology) — the Latin American theological movement that emerged from the Second Vatican Council and the 1968 Medellín conference — its key figures, its confrontation with the Vatican, and its later reception.
meta.dataQualityNote: This is a work-in-progress dataset compiled from public sources (see references). Liberation Theology is a politically and theologically contested subject: contested claims are attributed to their authors, never asserted in the site's own voice, and dates not yet verified against a primary source are flagged with a ? mark. Corrections against primary sources are welcome.

facts.label: What it is
facts.value: A family of Latin American theologies interpreting Christian faith from the experience of the poor and oppressed — a current of thought, not an organization with members or a headquarters.

facts.label: Emerged
facts.value: 1968–1971 — from the reception of the Second Vatican Council at the 1968 Medellín conference (CELAM II) to Gustavo Gutiérrez's foundational book (Lima, 1971).

facts.label: Foundational text
facts.value: Gustavo Gutiérrez, 'Teología de la liberación. Perspectivas' (Lima, 1971; English: 'A Theology of Liberation', Orbis, 1973).

facts.label: Core theme
facts.value: The 'preferential option for the poor', consolidated by the CELAM conferences of Medellín (1968) and Puebla (1979).

facts.label: Vatican response
facts.value: Two CDF instructions signed under Cardinal Ratzinger: Libertatis Nuntius (1984), criticizing borrowings from Marxist analysis, and Libertatis Conscientia (1986), developing a positive theology of freedom and liberation.

facts.label: Strongest base
facts.value: Brazil — through the CEBs (comunidades eclesiais de base) and bishops such as Hélder Câmara and Pedro Casaldáliga; estimates of CEB numbers at the 1980s peak vary widely between sources.

events.title: CELAM founded
events.text: At the First General Conference of the Latin American Episcopate (Rio de Janeiro, 25 July–4 August 1955), convoked by Pius XII through the apostolic letter 'Ad Ecclesiam Christi', the bishops resolved to create the Latin American Episcopal Council; Pius XII approved its canonical constitution on 2 November 1955, with headquarters in Bogotá.

events.title: ISAL founded
events.text: Iglesia y Sociedad en América Latina (ISAL) — the radicalizing Protestant 'church and society' network (Rubem Alves, Richard Shaull, José Míguez Bonino) that was a precursor stream to liberation theology — grew out of the First Latin American Consultation on Church and Society, held at Huampaní, Peru, in July 1961 and backed by the World Council of Churches; the consultation created the Junta Latinoamericana de Iglesia y Sociedad. Scholarship places the founding consultation at Huampaní; the occasional 'Huancayo' attribution is unsupported.

events.title: Second Vatican Council opens
events.text: The Council (1962–1965) opens the space that Latin American bishops later 'receive' at Medellín.

events.title: Camilo Torres laicized; leads the Frente Unido
events.text: Having co-founded Latin America's first sociology faculty at the Universidad Nacional after studying at Louvain, Torres asked to be reduced to lay status amid conflict with Cardinal Concha and gave his final Mass at the Church of San Diego, devoting himself to the Frente Unido popular-front movement whose weekly newspaper had launched that August. Months later he joined the ELN guerrilla.

events.title: Pact of the Catacombs
events.text: Three weeks before Vatican II closed, some 40 bishops celebrated Mass in the Catacombs of Domitilla and signed the 'Pact of the Catacombs', pledging to live simply and make a 'church of the poor' central to their ministry. Hélder Câmara was a leading promoter; over 500 bishops later added their signatures. The pact became closely associated with the emerging current.

events.title: Death of Camilo Torres
events.text: The Colombian priest and sociologist, who had joined the ELN guerrilla, is killed in his first combat; later invoked as a precursor symbol, though his choice of armed struggle was not representative of liberation theologians.

events.title: Populorum Progressio
events.text: Paul VI's encyclical on 'the development of peoples', a key reference for the movement.

events.title: Gutiérrez's Chimbote lecture
events.text: Asked to speak on a 'theology of development', Gustavo Gutiérrez reframes the topic as 'Hacia una teología de la liberación'.

events.title: CELAM II (Medellín)
events.text: Paul VI opens the conference in Bogotá on 24 August (first papal visit to Latin America); working sessions in Medellín 26 August – 6 September adopt the language of 'institutionalized violence' and the option for the poor.

events.title: Rubem Alves, 'A Theology of Human Hope'
events.text: Alves's Princeton dissertation, defended in 1968 as 'Towards a Theology of Liberation', was published in 1969 as 'A Theology of Human Hope'; his publisher required the retitling for the US market. Harvey Cox later observed that but for the change, Alves rather than Gutiérrez might be remembered as the 'father of liberation theology'.

events.title: Gutiérrez publishes 'Teología de la liberación'
events.text: The movement's foundational text; English translation 'A Theology of Liberation' follows in 1973 (Orbis).

events.title: Casaldáliga becomes bishop
events.text: Paul VI named Pedro Casaldáliga prelate of São Félix and titular bishop of Altava on 27 August 1971; he received episcopal ordination on 23 October 1971 (principal consecrator Fernando Gomes dos Santos, Archbishop of Goiânia), beginning his emblematic 'church of the poor' prelature in the Araguaia (served 1971–2005).

events.title: Christians for Socialism encounter
events.text: First Latin American Encounter of Cristianos por el Socialismo (~400 delegates); the movement had begun in April 1971 when some 80 Chilean priests declared support for socialism.

events.title: López Trujillo elected CELAM secretary-general
events.text: Alfonso López Trujillo was elected secretary-general of CELAM at Sucre, Bolivia, on 22 November 1972; from this post (and the CELAM presidency from 1979) he became, in the widely held reading, the leading intra-Latin-American institutional opponent of liberation theology.

events.title: Assmann's 'Teología desde la praxis de la liberación'
events.text: Hugo Assmann published (Ediciones Sígueme, Salamanca) 'Teología desde la praxis de la liberación', a reworking of earlier texts that became one of the founding and most radical methodological statements of the current, proposing the liberating praxis of the poor as the starting point of theology.

events.title: Chilean bishops ban Christians for Socialism
events.text: The Chilean episcopate's April 1973 plenary condemned clergy participation in Cristianos por el Socialismo; the resulting declaration 'Fe cristiana y actuación política', signed by Cardinal Raúl Silva Henríquez, was delayed by the 11 September coup and published on 16 October 1973, barring priests and religious from the movement. The substantive decision thus predates the coup, even though the formal text appeared after it.

events.title: First Interecclesial Meeting of the CEBs
events.text: The first national encounter of comunidades eclesiais de base gathered about 70 delegates from a dozen states, growing out of a January 1974 conversation involving Frei Betto, auxiliary bishop Luiz Gonzaga Fernandes and historian Eduardo Hoornaert. Betto became a leading organizer of these periodic meetings and a widely read popularizer of the CEBs. (Movement records; exact dates not archival.)

events.title: Murder of Rutilio Grande
events.text: Jesuit priest Rutilio Grande, an advocate of peasant organizing and a friend of the newly appointed Archbishop Romero, was ambushed and killed with two companions. Many biographers (following Brockman) treat the killing as the catalyst of Romero's public turn; others (Morozzo della Rocca) argue his stance evolved from his pastoral formation. Both readings are attributed.

events.title: Somoza's National Guard destroys the Solentiname community
events.text: After Solentiname members joined the FSLN assault on the San Carlos barracks (13 October 1977), Somoza's National Guard razed Cardenal's contemplative art-and-Gospel community in retaliation and he went into exile in Costa Rica. The community's collective Gospel readings had been published as 'El Evangelio en Solentiname', a landmark text of the current.

events.title: CELAM III (Puebla)
events.text: John Paul II's opening address (28 January) warns against 're-readings' of Christ as political revolutionary while affirming the defense of human dignity; the conference document consolidates the 'preferential option for the poor'.

events.title: Assassination of Óscar Romero
events.text: A day after a homily ordering soldiers, 'in the name of God', to cease the repression, Romero was shot dead while celebrating Mass. The 1993 UN Truth Commission for El Salvador found that former Major Roberto D'Aubuisson had given the order to kill the archbishop; the finding is attributed to that report.

events.title: Boff publishes 'Igreja: Carisma e Poder'
events.text: Franciscan theologian Leonardo Boff published 'Igreja: Carisma e Poder — Ensaios de eclesiologia militante' (Editora Vozes), whose critique of the Church's institutional power structure prompted the CDF investigation that led to the 1984 colloquy and the 1985 Notification; it appeared in English as 'Church: Charism and Power' (Crossroad, 1985).

events.title: John Paul II reprimands Ernesto Cardenal
events.text: As John Paul II arrived in Nicaragua on 4 March 1983, Culture Minister Cardenal knelt to greet him; the pope wagged his finger and admonished him to 'regularize' his situation with the Church — an image emblematic of the Vatican's clash with priests in the Sandinista government.

events.title: Cardenal suspended a divinis
events.text: Ernesto Cardenal is suspended from priestly ministry under John Paul II for holding government office.

events.title: CDF Instruction 'Libertatis Nuntius'
events.text: The Instruction on Certain Aspects of the 'Theology of Liberation', signed by Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger, warns against the uncritical borrowing of Marxist analysis while not condemning the theme of liberation itself.

events.title: Boff's colloquy with the CDF over 'Church: Charism and Power'
events.text: Leonardo Boff was summoned to a colloquy at the Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith, meeting for about two hours with its prefect, Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger, over his 1981 book 'Church: Charism and Power'. Brazilian cardinals Aloísio Lorscheider and Paulo Evaristo Arns attended in support; a Notification and a period of imposed silence followed in 1985.

events.title: Segundo's 'Theology and the Church' answers Cardinal Ratzinger
events.text: Juan Luis Segundo published 'Theology and the Church: A Response to Cardinal Ratzinger and a Warning to the Whole Church', a book-length rebuttal to the CDF's 1984 Instruction 'Libertatis Nuntius', arguing the critique rested on a dualistic theology at odds with Vatican II and missed what liberation theologians actually taught. It became a key primary document of the reception debate (reviewed by Avery Dulles, 1986).

events.title: Notification on Leonardo Boff
events.text: The CDF issues a Notification on Boff's 'Church: Charism and Power'; Boff is subsequently subjected to a period of imposed silence, lifted in 1986.

events.title: 'Fidel e a Religião' (Fidel and Religion)
events.text: Frei Betto's long interviews with Fidel Castro, conducted in May 1985, were published as 'Fidel y la religión' (Havana, 1985; English 'Fidel and Religion', 1987). Some observers credit the book with helping open a dialogue between the Cuban revolution and religious believers; that assessment is contested and attributed, not asserted.

events.title: CDF Instruction 'Libertatis Conscientia'
events.text: The Instruction on Christian Freedom and Liberation gives a more positive treatment of liberation themes.

events.title: John Paul II's letter to the Brazilian bishops
events.text: In a letter dated 9 April 1986 to the Brazilian Episcopal Conference (CNBB), John Paul II writes that — developed in fidelity to Church doctrine and the two recent CDF Instructions — 'the theology of liberation is not only opportune, but useful and necessary' ('la teologia della liberazione è non solo opportuna, ma utile e necessaria'), a passage cited by the movement's defenders.

events.title: Boff brothers publish the liberation-theology primer
events.text: Clodovis and Leonardo Boff co-published 'Como fazer teologia da libertação' (Vozes), a widely translated methodological primer built on the see–judge–act method; it appeared in English as 'Introducing Liberation Theology' (Orbis, 1987). Clodovis would later, in 2007, publish a sharp epistemological self-critique of the movement.

events.title: UCA massacre
events.text: A Salvadoran army unit murders six Jesuits — including rector and philosopher Ignacio Ellacuría — and Elba and Celina Ramos at the Central American University.

events.title: Leonardo Boff leaves the priesthood and the Franciscans
events.text: In a letter dated 28 June 1992, Boff announced he was leaving priestly ministry and the Franciscan Order after renewed conflict with Vatican doctrinal authorities, writing that he was 'leaving the priestly ministry but not the church'.

events.title: CELAM IV (Santo Domingo)
events.text: Held under strong Roman oversight; widely read by commentators as marginalizing liberationist currents — a characterization attributed to those commentators.

events.title: Death of Hélder Câmara
events.text: Hélder Câmara died on 27 August 1999, aged 90. As Archbishop of Olinda e Recife he had been an internationally known advocate for the poor and for human rights under the dictatorship — subjected to press censorship (1968–1977) and repeatedly nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize — and is widely regarded as a pastoral precursor of the current.

events.title: Gutiérrez enters the Dominican Order
events.text: Gustavo Gutiérrez, a diocesan priest since his 1959 ordination in Lima, is received into the Order of Preachers (Dominicans); in the same period he takes up the John Cardinal O'Hara Professorship of Theology at the University of Notre Dame. (Some accounts give 1998–1999 for the entry; Notre Dame's own memorial states 2001.)

events.title: Notification on Jon Sobrino
events.text: The CDF Notification on Sobrino's christology books (dated 26 November 2006, published March 2007) finds 'erroneous or dangerous propositions' but imposes no formal silencing.

events.title: Archbishop bars Sobrino after the CDF Notification
events.text: After the March 2007 publication of the CDF Notification, Archbishop Fernando Sáenz Lacalle told reporters that Sobrino could not teach theology in the archdiocese unless he revised his positions. Per NCR reporting, Sobrino declined to sign or adhere, calling the procedures 'not always honest or very evangelical'; the Jesuit order said it saw no grounds for internal sanctions. (Accounts attributed to the reporting.)

events.title: CELAM V (Aparecida)
events.text: Opened by Benedict XVI; Cardinal Jorge Bergoglio chairs the final-document drafting committee — Aparecida is later described by commentators as the matrix of his pontificate.

events.title: Clodovis Boff's REB essay: 'the poor in the place of Christ'
events.text: Clodovis Boff publishes 'Teologia da Libertação e volta ao fundamento' (Revista Eclesiástica Brasileira 67/268), arguing that liberation theology 'started off on the right track but put the poor in the place of Christ', making the poor rather than Christ theology's first operative principle. He points to the 2007 Aparecida conference as a path back. (His position, attributed.)

events.title: Death of Hugo Assmann
events.text: Hugo Assmann died on 22 February 2008, aged 74. He had taught for over two decades at the Methodist University of Piracicaba (UNIMEP), where in his later years he turned to educational theory (in part with Jung Mo Sung) and to critiques of the 'idolatry of the market'. The full death date, previously unknown in this dataset, is confirmed by multiple obituaries.

events.title: Death of Alfonso López Trujillo
events.text: Cardinal Alfonso López Trujillo — CELAM secretary-general (from 1972) and president (from 1979), later president of the Pontifical Council for the Family — died in Rome on 19 April 2008; his funeral Mass was celebrated by Benedict XVI in St Peter's Basilica on 23 April.

events.title: Leonardo Boff's public reply to his brother
events.text: Leonardo Boff answers in REB, calling his brother's text a cause of 'perplexity and disturbance', warning it would be used to finish off liberation theology, and holding that 'to encounter the poor is to encounter Christ'. The exchange is the well-documented fraternal dispute over the current's method; both positions are attributed to their authors.

events.title: Election of Pope Francis
events.text: Jorge Bergoglio becomes the first Latin American pope.

events.title: Francis receives Gutiérrez
events.text: Francis meets privately with Gustavo Gutiérrez, brought by CDF prefect Gerhard Müller, Gutiérrez's friend and co-author — widely read as a rehabilitation-adjacent gesture, a reading attributed to commentators.

events.title: Death of Rubem Alves
events.text: Rubem Alves died on 19 July 2014 in Campinas, aged 80. Remembered by the World Council of Churches as a foundational thinker of Latin American liberation theology, he had participated in ISAL and been sought by the dictatorship; his later output spanned educational theory, chronicles, children's books and psychotherapy.

events.title: 'On the Side of the Poor': Gutiérrez and Cardinal Müller
events.text: Orbis Books publishes 'On the Side of the Poor: The Theology of Liberation', pairing essays by Gutiérrez with those of Cardinal Gerhard Ludwig Müller — Prefect of the Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith (2012–2017) and a former student of Gutiérrez who had spent summers working in Peru (the material first appeared in German as 'An der Seite der Armen', 2004). Müller praised Gutiérrez as 'one of the great theologians of our time'; commentators read the collaboration as a public marker of a thaw between Rome and liberation theology — characterizations attributed to their authors.

events.title: Romero recognized as a martyr
events.text: Pope Francis authorized a decree recognizing that Romero was killed 'in hatred of the faith' (in odium fidei), declaring him a martyr and clearing the way for beatification without a certified miracle, after years of debate over whether he had died for the faith or for political stances.

events.title: Romero beatified
events.text: Óscar Romero is declared a martyr (February 2015) and beatified.

events.title: Francis's letter for Gutiérrez's 90th birthday
events.text: In a letter dated 28 May 2018, ahead of Gutiérrez's 90th birthday (8 June), Pope Francis thanks him for his 'theological service' and his 'preferential love for the poor and the discarded of society', encouraging him to continue his prayer and service. Together with their 2013 meeting, the letter is widely cited as evidence of warming relations under Francis; whether it amounts to a rehabilitation of liberation theology is contested (see disambiguation).

events.title: Romero canonized
events.text: Francis canonizes Óscar Romero.

events.title: Cardenal's suspension lifted
events.text: Francis grants Ernesto Cardenal 'absolution from all canonical censures', ending the 1984 suspension.

events.title: Death of Ernesto Cardenal
events.text: The priest-poet of Solentiname dies at 95.

events.title: Death of Pedro Casaldáliga
events.text: Dom Pedro Casaldáliga died on 8 August 2020, aged 92, at the Claretian centre in Batatais, São Paulo; his body was taken to São Félix do Araguaia for burial. The death date, previously unverified in this dataset, is confirmed by Vatican News, the CNBB and Brazilian press.

events.title: Spanish court convicts ex-colonel Montano for the UCA massacre
events.text: Spain's National Court convicted former Salvadoran colonel Inocente Orlando Montano on five counts of murder 'of a terrorist nature' for his role in the 1989 killing of the Jesuits and two women, sentencing him to 133 years. Prosecuted under universal jurisdiction because five victims held Spanish citizenship, he had been extradited from the United States in 2017 — the first criminal conviction for the massacre.

events.title: First Ecclesial Assembly of Latin America
events.text: CELAM convenes bishops together with laity (over 1,000 participants), presented as being in continuity with Aparecida.

events.title: Death of Gustavo Gutiérrez
events.text: The 'father of liberation theology' dies at 96 in the Dominican convent in Lima.

events.title: Election of Leo XIV
events.text: After the death of Francis (21 April 2025), Robert Prevost — for decades a bishop and missionary in Peru — is elected; liberation theologians' expressions of hope, and debate about his stance, are attributed to the sources reporting them.

events.title: Clodovis Boff's open letter to CELAM
events.text: The former liberation-theology systematizer, long an internal critic, publicly criticizes what he calls an 'excess' of social emphasis by the Latin American bishops, sparking controversy.

events.title: Leo XIV's 'Dilexi te'
events.text: The first apostolic exhortation of Leo XIV, on love for the poor, begun under Francis — read by some commentators as continuity with liberationist emphases, a reading others dispute; both attributed.

events.title: Camilo Torres's remains identified
events.text: The ELN reports finding and hands over the remains of the guerrilla priest, identified some 60 years after his 1966 death.

events.title: Camilo Torres's remains handed over for reburial
events.text: Sixty years after his death, and after the state missing-persons unit (UBPD) confirmed the identification by DNA, Torres's remains were handed to activist priest Javier Giraldo following a Mass at the Universidad Nacional, where President Gustavo Petro backed reburial on the campus Torres had served as chaplain.

figures.name: Gustavo Gutiérrez
figures.role: Peruvian priest (b. Lima, 8 June 1928; ordained 1959), who entered the Dominican Order (Order of Preachers) in 2001 and taught at the University of Notre Dame as John Cardinal O'Hara Professor of Theology; 'father of liberation theology' and author of the movement's foundational 1971 book. Never formally condemned by the Vatican — a point he himself insisted on — and in later years publicly esteemed by Cardinal Gerhard Müller (his former student and a CDF prefect) and by Pope Francis.

figures.name: Leonardo Boff
figures.role: Leonardo Boff (b. 1938, Concórdia, Brazil) is a Brazilian theologian and former Franciscan priest, a leading figure of Latin American liberation theology. Silenced by the Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith over 'Church: Charism and Power' (1981), he left the priesthood and Franciscan order in 1992 and later turned to eco-theology, base ecclesial communities and integral ecology.

figures.name: Clodovis Boff
figures.role: Clodovis Boff, OSM (b. 1944, Brazil), Brazilian Servite priest and systematic theologian, a co-founder of liberation theology's method who co-wrote 'Introducing Liberation Theology' with his brother Leonardo and authored 'Theology and Praxis'. From 2007 he publicly broke with the current's method, arguing it had put the poor in the place of Christ; he has taught at PUC-Rio and PUC-Paraná.

figures.name: Jon Sobrino
figures.role: Jon Sobrino (b. 1938, Barcelona) is a Spanish-Basque Jesuit who went to El Salvador in the late 1950s and became a leading christologist at the Universidad Centroamericana (UCA). His liberation christology reads Jesus 'from the victims'. Abroad during the 16 November 1989 UCA massacre, he survived and chronicled its martyrs and Óscar Romero; a 2006/07 CDF Notification found 'erroneous or dangerous' propositions in his work.

figures.name: Ignacio Ellacuría
figures.role: Ignacio Ellacuría (1930–1989), Basque-born Jesuit philosopher and theologian in El Salvador and a disciple of Xavier Zubiri. He developed a 'philosophy of historical reality' and the method of 'historicization'. As rector of the UCA (from 1979) he pressed for a negotiated peace; he and seven others were killed by Salvadoran soldiers on 16 November 1989.

figures.name: Juan Luis Segundo
figures.role: Juan Luis Segundo (1925–1996), Uruguayan Jesuit theologian in Montevideo and a founding figure of the current. He developed the 'hermeneutic circle' method centred on ideological suspicion, holding that what liberates theology is its methodology rather than its content, and drew less on Marxist social analysis than some peers.

figures.name: Hugo Assmann
figures.role: Hugo Assmann (1933–2008), Brazilian theologian and educator, one of the earliest and most radical methodologists of the current. Exiled during Brazil's military dictatorship, he helped found the Departamento Ecuménico de Investigaciones (DEI) in Costa Rica. He later left the priesthood, married, and turned to critiques of market 'idolatry' and to pedagogy.

figures.name: Rubem Alves
figures.role: Brazilian Presbyterian theologian whose 1968 Princeton dissertation coined the phrase 'theology of liberation', making him the current's Protestant pioneer. Persecuted by both the military dictatorship and his own church, he later drifted from systematic theology toward psychoanalysis, chronicles, children's books and a poetic 'theopoetics'.

figures.name: Frei Betto (Carlos Alberto Libânio Christo)
figures.role: Brazilian Dominican friar, writer and popular educator (b. 1944, Belo Horizonte). Twice jailed under the military dictatorship (1964; c. 1969–1973), he became a leading organizer of Brazil's comunidades eclesiais de base and their interecclesial meetings. Author of 'Fidel e a Religião' (1985) and later a Fome Zero adviser (2003–04); his links to the Workers' Party and Cuba are documented in the sibling fsp project.

figures.name: Ernesto Cardenal
figures.role: Ernesto Cardenal (1925–2020), Nicaraguan poet and Catholic priest, a leading voice of the current in Central America. He founded the Solentiname contemplative-artistic community, whose Gospel dialogues became 'El Evangelio en Solentiname'. Sandinista Minister of Culture (1979–1987), suspended from ministry by John Paul II, he later broke with Daniel Ortega and had his suspension lifted by Pope Francis in 2019.

figures.name: Camilo Torres
figures.role: Colombian priest and sociologist who co-founded Latin America's first sociology faculty at the Universidad Nacional, led the Frente Unido movement, was laicized in 1965 and joined the ELN guerrilla, dying in his first combat in 1966. A precursor symbol who predates the current, not a liberation theologian: his choice of armed struggle was rejected by its main theologians such as Gustavo Gutiérrez.

figures.name: Hélder Câmara
figures.role: Brazilian Catholic prelate, Archbishop of Olinda e Recife (1964–1985) and co-founder of the CNBB (1952). A pastoral 'bishop of the poor' and human-rights advocate under the dictatorship, he is remembered as a precursor and moral inspiration of the current's social-justice strand rather than a systematic theologian; earlier an integralist sympathizer, he later renounced that stance.

figures.name: Óscar Romero
figures.role: Óscar Arnulfo Romero (1917–1980), Archbishop of San Salvador from 1977, the era's most prominent Salvadoran church voice against state repression before his assassination while saying Mass on 24 March 1980. Canonized in 2018 as a martyr killed in odium fidei, not as a liberation theologian; his own relationship to the current is debated among biographers.

figures.name: Pedro Casaldáliga
figures.role: Spanish-born Claretian missionary and bishop-prelate of São Félix do Araguaia (1971–2005). A pastoral figure and poet claimed by the current rather than a systematic theologian, he championed Indigenous people and landless peasants amid violent Araguaia land conflicts, co-founded the Comissão Pastoral da Terra, and drew Vatican scrutiny for his activism.

figures.name: John Paul II
figures.role: Pope 1978–2005. Warned against political 're-readings' of Christ at Puebla (1979) and presided over the disciplinary era of the 1980s, while his 1986 letter to the Brazilian bishops also used affirming language — both sides cited.

figures.name: Joseph Ratzinger / Benedict XVI
figures.role: CDF prefect 1981–2005 (signed Libertatis Nuntius, the Boff Notification, and Libertatis Conscientia), pope 2005–13 (opened Aparecida 2007).

figures.name: Alfonso López Trujillo
figures.role: Colombian cardinal (1935–2008). Elected CELAM secretary-general at Sucre, Bolivia, on 22 November 1972 and CELAM president from 1979; widely described as the leading intra-Latin-American institutional opponent of liberation theology. Later president of the Pontifical Council for the Family; died in Rome, 19 April 2008.

figures.name: Francis (Jorge Bergoglio)
figures.role: Pope 2013–2025. Chaired Aparecida's drafting committee (2007), received Gutiérrez (2013), canonized Romero (2018), restored Cardenal (2019); whether this amounts to a rehabilitation of liberation theology is debated — see disambiguation.

figures.name: Leo XIV (Robert Prevost)
figures.role: Pope from May 2025; spent decades as a missionary and bishop in Peru. Liberation theologians' expressions of hope about his pontificate, and his 'Dilexi te' (2025), are reported with debate about their significance.

organizations.name: CELAM (Consejo Episcopal Latinoamericano e Caribeño)
organizations.relation: The Latin American bishops' council whose general conferences frame the movement's history: Rio 1955, Medellín 1968, Puebla 1979, Santo Domingo 1992, Aparecida 2007, and the First Ecclesial Assembly 2021.

organizations.name: CEBs (Comunidades Eclesiais de Base)
organizations.relation: Grassroots ecclesial base communities, strongest in Brazil, that carried the movement's pastoral practice; estimates of their number at the 1980s peak vary widely between sources and are reported as ranges.
organizations.notes: Commentators state that CEB networks supplied cadres to Brazil's Workers' Party in the 1980s — an attributed, contested characterization.

organizations.name: Cristianos por el Socialismo (Christians for Socialism)
organizations.relation: Movement begun by Chilean priests supporting socialism (April 1971), with its first Latin American Encounter in Santiago in April 1972; banned for clergy by the Chilean bishops after the 1973 coup.

organizations.name: ISAL (Iglesia y Sociedad en América Latina)
organizations.relation: The Protestant 'church and society' network (journal 'Cristianismo y Sociedad') that formed a parallel precursor stream, with bridge figures such as Rubem Alves and Richard Shaull.

organizations.name: Derived and parallel liberation theologies
organizations.relation: Black theology (James Cone), feminist and womanist theologies, indigenous 'teología india', Dalit and Minjung theologies, and later ecological developments — related currents that dialogue with, but are distinct from, the Latin American movement.

map.note: Where the movement's story happened. 'Major center' marks countries with foundational events or leading figures documented in this chronology; 'documented presence' marks countries appearing through conferences or individual figures. The map reflects only what this dataset currently cites — not a claim about the movement's full geography.

countries.name: Peru
countries.note: Gutiérrez's home: the 1968 Chimbote lecture and the 1971 foundational book (Lima); decades later, the country where Leo XIV served as bishop.

countries.name: Brazil
countries.note: The movement's largest base: the CEBs, the Boff brothers, Frei Betto, Hélder Câmara, Casaldáliga, Rubem Alves; host of Aparecida 2007.

countries.name: El Salvador
countries.note: Óscar Romero (assassinated 1980, canonized 2018), the UCA Jesuits — Ellacuría, Sobrino — and the 1989 massacre.

countries.name: Nicaragua
countries.note: Ernesto Cardenal, Solentiname, and the Sandinista-era confrontation with John Paul II (1983 reprimand, 1984 suspension, 2019 restoration).

countries.name: Colombia
countries.note: Medellín 1968 (CELAM II), CELAM's Bogotá headquarters, precursor symbol Camilo Torres — and opponent Cardinal López Trujillo.

countries.name: Chile
countries.note: Christians for Socialism (1971–72) and its banning after the 1973 coup.

countries.name: Uruguay
countries.note: Juan Luis Segundo, author of 'The Liberation of Theology' (1975).

countries.name: Mexico
countries.note: Host of Puebla 1979 (CELAM III) and of the First Ecclesial Assembly (2021).

countries.name: Dominican Republic
countries.note: Host of Santo Domingo 1992 (CELAM IV), widely read by commentators as marginalizing liberationist currents.

countries.name: Argentina
countries.note: Home of the related but distinct 'teología del pueblo' that formed Jorge Bergoglio — kept apart from liberation theology in this dataset.

countries.name: Bolivia
countries.note: Reported site of the 1972 CELAM assembly that elected López Trujillo secretary-general (venue to verify).

disambiguation.note: Liberation Theology is politically contested terrain. The entries below record nuances that are easy to flatten; each characterization is attributed to its source.

items.title: A current, not an organization
items.text: Liberation theology has no founding charter, headquarters or membership roster. It is a family of theologies; chronology entries reference specific books, conferences and persons — never 'membership'.

items.title: 1984 ≠ blanket condemnation
items.text: Libertatis Nuntius (1984) criticizes 'certain aspects' — specifically the uncritical borrowing of Marxist analysis — and explicitly does not condemn liberation from poverty as a theme; Libertatis Conscientia (1986) develops a positive theology of liberation. Gutiérrez himself maintained that 'the Vatican never condemned the theology of liberation' — cited as his claim.

items.title: Different figures, different outcomes
items.text: Boff received a Notification and a silencing (1985); Sobrino a Notification without silencing (2006/07); Gutiérrez was scrutinized but never sanctioned. 'The Vatican condemned liberation theology' collapses three distinct outcomes.

items.title: Francis's stance is debated
items.text: Rehabilitative readings cite Aparecida, the 2013 Gutiérrez meeting, Romero's canonization and Cardenal's restoration; skeptical readings cite Bergoglio's formation in the distinct Argentine 'teología del pueblo' and analysts who say he distanced himself from figures like Boff. Both readings are attributed, not asserted.

items.title: Romero and Camilo Torres caveats
items.text: Romero was canonized as a martyr, not 'as a liberation theologian', and his relationship to the movement is debated; Camilo Torres predates the movement and chose armed struggle, which its main theologians did not advocate.

items.title: The PT / Foro de São Paulo link is an attributed claim
items.text: Claims that CEB networks fed Brazil's Workers' Party and that Frei Betto brokered the PT–Cuba relationship around the Foro de São Paulo's 1990 founding appear chiefly in critical literature and biographies. The sibling cronologia/fsp project documents this connection with the same attribute-don't-assert discipline.
